We were proclaiming that none shall enter Paradise except a believer, no naked person shall circumambulate the House, and whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, his matter or term is until four months; when the four months pass, then Allah and His Messenger are free from the polytheists, and no polytheist shall perform pilgrimage to this House after the year."

**I say:** There is clear inconsistency (اضطراب) in the content of this narration. First: because it includes the proclamation that "none shall enter Paradise except a believer," and it has already been mentioned that many Meccan and Medinan verses had been revealed regarding this years prior, heard by city-dwellers, Bedouins, polytheists, and believers alike; so what conceivable need was there to convey this to the gathered people?

Second: because the second proclamation—meaning his saying: "whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله..."—does not align with the contents of the verses nor with the contents of the numerous previous narrations. Furthermore, it places the repudiation (البراءة) after the passing of four months.

Third: for reasons we shall mention in the following.

In the same source, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* recorded from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه sent me during that pilgrimage among the announcers (مؤذنين) he sent on the Day of Sacrifice to announce at Mina: 'No polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after this year, and no naked person shall circumambulate the House.' Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Ali ibn Abi Talib رضي الله عنه after him and commanded him to announce *Bara’ah*. So Ali announced *Bara’ah* with us to the people of Mina on the Day of Sacrifice, and that no polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after the year, and no naked person shall circumambulate the House."

In *Tafsir al-Manar*, from al-Tirmidhi from Ibn Abbas: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr... [until he said]... then Ali stood during the Days of *Tashriq* and proclaimed: 'The protection (ذمة) of Allah and the protection of His Messenger are free from every polytheist; so travel the land for four months. No polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after the year, no naked person shall circumambulate the House, and none shall enter Paradise except every believer.' Ali continued to proclaim these, and when he became hoarse (بح), Abu Hurayrah stood and proclaimed them."

In the same source, also from Ahmad and al-Nasa’i—via Muhriz ibn Abi Hurayrah from his father, who said: "I was with Ali when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent him to Mecca with *Bara’ah*. We were proclaiming that no soul shall enter Paradise except a Muslim soul, no naked person shall circumambulate the House, and whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, his treaty is until its term, and no polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after the year. I continued to proclaim until my voice became raspy (صحل)."

**I say:** You have seen that what occurred in the narrations—despite their abundance—regarding the story of sending Ali and removing Abu Bakr, from the word of revelation (كلمة الوحي) brought by Gabriel to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, is his saying: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." Likewise, what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله mentioned when he answered Abu Bakr's question about the reason for his removal was the very text of what Allah, the Glorified, revealed to him, or his saying—which carries the same meaning—: "None shall discharge on my behalf except I or a man from me."

In any case, it is an absolute statement (كلام مطلق) that includes the discharge of *Bara’ah* and every divine ruling that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله needed to have discharged on his behalf by someone else. There is no evidence, neither from the texts of the narrations nor otherwise, indicating that this was exclusive to *Bara’ah*. It has become clear that the prohibition of circumambulating the House naked, the prohibition of polytheists performing pilgrimage after that year, and the granting of a grace period to those with or without treaties—all of these are divine rulings revealed in the Quran. So what is the meaning of referring their matter to Abu Bakr, or Abu Hurayrah proclaiming them alone, or his proclaiming *Bara’ah* and the other mentioned rulings in the gathering if Ali عليه‌السلام became hoarse until his voice became raspy from the frequency of the proclamation? If it were permissible for Abu Hurayrah to perform this in such a state, why was it not permissible for Abu Bakr?

Indeed, some commentators like Ibn Kathir and his peers have devised a justification here to explain what these narrations contain in order to defend them. They argue that his saying: "None shall discharge on my behalf except I or a man from me" is specific to the discharge of *Bara’ah* only, without including the other rulings that Ali عليه‌السلام was proclaiming. They claim that his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appointment of Ali to convey the verses of *Bara’ah* to the gathered people was only because it was the custom of the Arabs (عادة العرب) that a treaty is not broken except by the one who contracted it or a man from his household (أهل بيته). Observing this current custom is what prompted the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to take *Bara’ah*—which contained the breaking of the polytheists' treaties—from Abu Bakr and hand it to Ali, in order to preserve the Arab tradition (السنة العربية) so that it would be discharged on his behalf by someone from his household.

They said: This is the meaning of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying when Abu Bakr asked him: "O Messenger of Allah, has something been revealed regarding me?" He replied: "No, but none shall discharge on my behalf except I or a man from me." Its meaning is: "I only removed you and appointed Ali for this so as not to break this current Arab tradition."

Therefore, [they argue] Abu Bakr was not separated from his role; he had been entrusted with the leadership of the pilgrimage, and Abu Bakr had announcers who proclaimed these rulings, such as Abu Hurayrah and other men whose names are not mentioned in the narrations. He was the one in charge of this matter. Thus, it is reported in some narrations that he delivered a sermon at Mina, and when he finished his sermon, he turned to Ali and said: "Stand, O Ali, and discharge the message of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله." This is what they mentioned and how they justified the narrations.

The critical researcher, when reviewing these verses and narrations and then reflecting on the theological disputes (المشاجرات الكلامية) that occurred between the two groups—the Sunnis and the Shi‘a—regarding the matter of superiority (الأفضلية), will not doubt that they have mixed between exegetical research (البحث التفسيري)—whose function is to obtain the meanings of the Quranic verses—and traditional research (البحث الروائي)—whose function is to critique the meanings of the hadiths and distinguish the weak from the sound—and theological research (البحث الكلامي), which considers whether Abu Bakr is superior to Ali or Ali superior to Abu Bakr, and whether the leadership of the pilgrimage is superior or the mission of conveying the verses of *Bara’ah*, and who held the leadership of the pilgrimage then, Abu Bakr or Ali? As for theological research, we are not engaged in it in this place, as it is outside our purpose. As for traditional or exegetical research regarding how the verses relate to their occasions of revelation (أسباب نزولها) concerning the meanings of the verses, what ought to be said in view of it is that they erred in this justification.

I wonder, from where did they accept that this sentence revealed by Gabriel—"None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you"—is restricted to the breaking of the treaty and does not indicate more than that? There is no evidence for this from transmission or reason. The sentence is most clearly apparent in that whatever the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was required to discharge, it is not permissible for anyone to discharge it except him or a man from him besides him—whether it was the breaking of a treaty from Allah's side as in the case of *Bara’ah*, or another divine ruling that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was required to discharge and convey.

This is distinct from the types of missions (الرسالة) from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he was not required to discharge personally, such as the letters he sent to kings, nations, and peoples inviting them to Islam, as well as other missions where he would send men from among the believers to the people regarding matters of their religion, leaderships (الإمارات), governorships (الولايات), and the like.

There is a clear difference between these matters and *Bara’ah* and its likes. For what the verses of *Bara’ah* contain—and examples like the prohibition of circumambulating naked and the prohibition of polytheists performing pilgrimage after the year—are initial divine rulings (أحكام إلهية ابتدائية) that had not yet been communicated or discharged to those who must receive them, namely the polytheists in Mecca and the pilgrims among them. There is no mission from Allah in this except for His Messenger. As for other instances where the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was content with sending messengers for communication, they were matters in which he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had already finished the original communication and discharge by conveying it to those he could among those present—such as the call to Islam and other laws of the religion—and he would say: "Let the witness among you convey to the absent." Then, if the need arose to convey it to some who would not normally be reached by the ruling, or if the mere reaching of the message would have no effect unless it was given importance through a letter or a messenger, he would resort at that time to a mission or a letter, as in the invitation to kings.

Let the impartial researcher reflect on his saying: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." It was said: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you," and it did not say: "None shall discharge except you or a man from you," which would imply a shared mission. Nor did it say: "None shall discharge from you except a man from you," which would include all other missions he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entrusted to any of the righteous believers. The meaning of his saying: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you" is that the missionary matters (الأمور الرسالية) which you yourself must undertake shall not be undertaken by anyone else in your stead except a man from you; that is, no one succeeds you in what is incumbent upon you—such as initial discharge (التأدية الابتدائية)—except a man from you.

Furthermore, I wonder what prompted them to ignore the word of revelation (كلمة الوحي), which is the word of Allah brought down by Gabriel to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you," and mention in its place that "it was the current tradition among the Arabs that a treaty is not broken except by the one who contracted it or a man from his household"? That Arab tradition (السنة العربية) of which there is no report in their days or battles, nor any trace except what Ibn Kathir mentioned and attributed to the scholars when discussing the verses of *Bara’ah*!

Moreover, if there were a pre-Islamic Arab tradition (سنة عربية جاهلية) of this description, what is its weight in Islam and what is its value to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, when he was abrogating a pre-Islamic tradition every day and breaking a national custom at every moment? It was not among the noble character traits or useful traditions and customs, but rather a tribal disposition (سليقة قبائلية) resembling the dispositions of the nobility. He (ص) said on the day of the Conquest of Mecca at the Kaaba, as narrated by the biographers: "Indeed, every claim of privilege (مأثرة), blood, or property is under these two feet of mine—except the custodianship of the House and the providing of water to the pilgrims."

Furthermore, if it were a non-reprehensible Arab tradition, had the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله become heedless of it and forgotten it when he handed the verses to Abu Bakr and sent him, and he set out for Mecca, until when he was partway there, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remembered what he had forgotten, or someone with him reminded him of what he had neglected and overlooked regarding a matter that was obligatory to observe? And he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the supreme example in noble character and in considering what must be considered of prudence and good management. How was it permissible for these "reminders" to be heedless of that, when it is not among the matters that are usually overlooked or hidden? Forgetting it would be like a warrior forgetting his weapon.

Was it by revelation from Allah to him that he must not cancel this noble Arab tradition, and that it was one of the legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) in this chapter, and that it is forbidden for the guardian of the Muslims' affairs to break a treaty except by himself or by the hand of one of his household? What is the meaning of such a ruling?

Or was it an ethical ruling (حكم أخلاقي) he was forced to consider because the polytheists would not accept this breaking [of the treaty] unless they heard it from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله himself or from one of his household? Yet the dominance that day belonged to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) over them, and the reins were in his hand, not theirs, and communication is communication.

Or was it that the believers addressed by His saying: **عاهَدْتُمْ** (you have made a treaty) and His saying: **وَأَذانٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى النَّاسِ** (and a proclamation from Allah and His Messenger to the people) and His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (then slay the polytheists) would not consider this breaking to be a breaking unless they heard it from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or from one of his household, even if they knew of the breaking by hearing the verses from Abu Bakr?

If it were so, how did those who heard it from Abu Hurayrah—who was proclaiming it until his voice became raspy—accept it and consider it a breaking? Was Abu Hurayrah closer to Ali and more intimately connected to him than Abu Bakr was to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله? The truth is that these narrations recounting the proclamation of Abu Hurayrah and others are not sound and should not be relied upon.

The author of *al-Manar* said in his *Tafsir*: "The sum of the narrations indicates that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appointed Abu Bakr as leader over the pilgrimage in the ninth year and commanded him to inform the polytheists attending the pilgrimage that they would be barred from it after that year. Then he sent Ali after him to inform them on his behalf of the repudiation of their absolute treaties and to give them a grace period of four months to consider their affairs, and that the fixed-term treaties would end at their specified time; and to recite to them the verses containing the matter of repudiating treaties and what relates to it from the beginning of Surah *Bara’ah*."

"These are forty or thirty-three verses, and what is mentioned in some narrations of hesitation between thirty and forty is an expression in tens while ignoring the fraction of increase or decrease."

"This is because it was the custom of the Arabs that treaties and their repudiation only come from the one who contracted them or one of his close kinsmen (عصبته القريبة), and Ali was specifically for that while the leadership of the pilgrimage remained with Abu Bakr, who assisted him in that and commanded some companions like Abu Hurayrah to assist him." [End of quote].

He also said: "Some of the Shi‘a exaggerate this merit for Ali عليه‌السلام as is their habit, and add to it what is not supported by narration nor confirmed by reason, using it to argue for his superiority over Abu Bakr رضي الله عنهما and his being more entitled to the caliphate than him. They claim that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله removed Abu Bakr from conveying Surah *Bara’ah* because Gabriel commanded him so, and that none conveys on his behalf except he or a man from him; and they do not restrict this negation to the conveyance of the repudiation of treaties and what relates to it, but make it general for the entire matter of religion."

"This is despite the profusion of authentic reports (استفاضة الأخبار الصحيحة) regarding the obligation of conveying the religion upon all Muslims, like jihad in its protection and defense, and its being a duty (فريضة) and not just a merit. Among these is his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying in the Farewell Pilgrimage within earshot of thousands of people: 'Lo, let the witness convey to the absent,' which is repeated in the two *Sahihs* and elsewhere. In some narrations from Ibn Abbas: 'By Him in Whose hand is my soul, it is his testament to his community: "let the witness convey to the absent,"' etc. And the hadith: 'Convey from me even if it be a single verse,' narrated by al-Bukhari in his *Sahih* and al-Tirmidhi. Were it not for that, Islam would not have spread so rapidly in the world."

"Rather, some of them claimed—as has been said—that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله removed Abu Bakr from the leadership of the pilgrimage and appointed Ali over it. This is a blatant slander (بهتان صريح) contrary to all narrations in a practical matter known to the elite and the commoners alike."

"The truth is that Ali, may Allah ennoble his face, was charged with conveying a specific matter, and in that pilgrimage, he was a follower of Abu Bakr in his general leadership in establishing the general social pillar of Islam, to the point that Abu Bakr would specify for him the time in which he should convey that, saying: 'O Ali, stand and convey the message of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله,' as previously stated in the authentic narrations. He also commanded some companions to assist him in this conveyance, as seen in the hadith of Abu Hurayrah in the two *Sahihs* and elsewhere."

Then he continued his discourse and argued from Abu Bakr's leadership in that pilgrimage—and added to it his leading the prayer in the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله place shortly before his death—for his precedence and superiority over all the companions and everyone else. [End of quote].

As for his saying: "despite the profusion of reports regarding the obligation of conveying the religion upon all Muslims..." to the end of what he said, it reveals that he did not properly grasp the meaning of the word of revelation: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." He did not distinguish between our saying: "None shall discharge from you except a man from you" and his saying: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." Thus, he imagined that the speech in its absolute sense prevents any religious conveyance undertaken by someone other than the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or a man from him. He countered this with the profusion of reports regarding the obligation of conveying the religion upon all Muslims and used it to restrict the absolute nature of "None shall discharge on your behalf...", making it specific to the conveyance of the repudiation of the treaty after transforming the divine ruling into a pre-Islamic Arab tradition.

His confusion over the meaning of the word led him to imagine that maintaining the speech in its absolute sense stems from heedlessness of a matter that is like a necessity among the general Muslims—namely, the obligation of general conveyance—to the point that he argued for this using what is in the two *Sahihs* and elsewhere of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: "Let the witness convey to the absent." You have already seen what the true meaning of the word of revelation is.

As for his saying: "Rather, some of them claimed—as has been said—that he removed Abu Bakr from the leadership of the pilgrimage and appointed Ali over it... this is a blatant slander contrary to all narrations in a practical matter known to the commoners and the elite," this is not a claim by "some" nor a slander as he alleged; rather, it is a narration reported by the Shi‘a, and we have cited it among the preceding narrations.

Deeply involving oneself in the matter of leadership (الإمارة) is not what concerns us in understanding the meaning of his saying: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." For the leadership of the pilgrimage—whether it was correctly attributed to Abu Bakr or Ali, and whether it indicated merit or not—is merely one of the branches of general Islamic authority (الولاية الإسلامية العامة), whose function is to manage the vital affairs of the Islamic society and implement religious rulings and laws. It has no authority over the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) and the materials of revelation descending from heaven regarding the matter of religion.

It is the authority (ولاية) of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; he appoints Abu Bakr or Ali one day for the leadership of the pilgrimage, and another day he appoints Usama over Abu Bakr and the general companions in his army, and another day he appoints Ibn Umm Maktum over Medina while there are those superior to him in it, and he appoints this one over Mecca after its conquest, and that one over Yemen, and that one over the matter of charities. He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appointed Abu Dujanah al-Sa‘idi or Siba‘ ibn ‘Arfatah al-Ghifari—according to what is in the *Sirah* of Ibn Hisham—over Medina during the year of the Farewell Pilgrimage, while Abu Bakr was in it and did not go out for the pilgrimage, as narrated by al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa’i, and others. This only indicates his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recognition of the competence of the one he appointed for the task to undertake it and manage its affairs.

As for the heavenly revelation (الوحي السماوي) with the gnosis and laws it contains, neither the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله nor anyone below him has any hand in it or influence over it from the general authority he has over the affairs of the Islamic society—whether by absolute or restricted application, endorsement, abrogation, or otherwise. Nor is it governed by a national tradition or a current custom such that it would necessitate its application to what accords with it, or the standing of a kinsman (العصبة) in place of the person in what concerns him.

Mixing between the two domains causes divine gnosis to descend from the peak of its height and nobility to the depths of social thoughts, where nothing governs except customs, traditions, and conventions. Thus, man returns to interpreting the realities of gnosis according to what common thoughts can encompass, magnifying what society magnifies rather than what Allah has magnified, and belittling what people belittle, until one says regarding the meaning of the word of revelation that it is a "respected Arab custom."

If you reflect on this story—the taking of the verses of *Bara’ah* from Abu Bakr and giving them to Ali as the narrations recount it—you will find in it a strange matter of laxity among the narrators and their expansiveness in preserving the story with its specificities—if it was not based on another purpose. In some of them—which are the majority—he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr with the verses, then sent Ali and commanded him to take them from him and recite them to the people, so Abu Bakr returned, etc. In some, he sent Abu Bakr with the leadership of the pilgrimage, then sent Ali after him with the verses of *Bara’ah*. In some, Abu Bakr commanded him to convey and commanded some companions to share with him in the proclamation, until the matter reached what al-Tabari and others narrated from Mujahid regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (A repudiation from Allah and His Messenger to those of the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty)—to the people of the treaty: Khuza‘ah, Mudlij, and whoever had a treaty or otherwise. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله arrived from Tabuk when he finished it and intended to perform the pilgrimage, then said: "Polytheists attend the House circumambulating naked, so I do not wish to perform pilgrimage until that is no longer the case." So he sent Abu Bakr and Ali, and they went around among the people at Dhu al-Majaz and in their places where they used to trade and throughout the entire season, and they notified the people of the treaty that they were safe for four months—which are the consecutive months that pass: twenty from the end of *Dhu al-Hijjah* until ten have passed of *Rabi‘ al-Awwal*—then he made a treaty with them and notified all the people of fighting until they die.

If this is the case, what is the meaning of his saying: "a blatant slander contrary to all narrations in a practical matter known to the commoners and the elite"? If he means: known to the commoners and the elite in the era of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله among those who witnessed the matter or heard it from those who witnessed and described it, then how does that benefit us?

And if he means: that the commoners and the elite among those who succeeded the era of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or succeeded those who succeeded them knew that and no one doubted it, then this is the state of the narrations reported from them—they do not agree on a single word.

Among them are those that recount that Ali was specifically for the discharge of *Bara’ah*, and others indicating that Abu Bakr shared it with him, and others indicating that Abu Hurayrah shared in the discharge with him, along with other men not named in the narrations.

Among them are those indicating that the verses were nine, others ten, others sixteen, others thirty, others thirty-three, others thirty-seven, others forty, and others the whole of Surah *Bara’ah*.

Among them are those indicating that Abu Bakr went on his way as leader of the pilgrims, and others that he returned—to the point that some like Ibn Kathir interpreted it as him returning after completing the pilgrimage, and others that he returned to ask the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the reason for his removal; and in the narration of Anas to follow, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr with *Bara’ah* then called him and took it from him.

Among them are those indicating that the pilgrimage occurred in *Dhu al-Hijjah* and that the Day of the Great Pilgrimage was the entirety of the days of that pilgrimage, or the Day of Arafah, or the Day of Sacrifice, or the day following the Day of Sacrifice, or otherwise; and others that Abu Bakr performed the pilgrimage in that year in *Dhu al-Qa‘dah*.

Among them are those indicating that the months of travel (أشهر السياحة) begin from *Shawwal*, others from *Dhu al-Qa‘dah*, others from the tenth of *Dhu al-Hijjah*, others from the eleventh of *Dhu al-Hijjah*, and so on.

Among them are those indicating that the sacred months (الأشهر الحرم) are *Dhu al-Qa‘dah*, *Dhu al-Hijjah*, and *al-Muharram* of that year, and others that they are the months of travel beginning from the day of proclamation or the day of revelation.

This is the state of the discrepancy in the narrations; despite this, how can the claim be sustained that it is a matter known to the commoners and the elite? Some of the previous possibilities, although they are sayings of the early commentators, are treated by commentators as suspended narrations (روايات موقوفة).

As for his saying: "The truth is that Ali was charged with conveying a specific matter and in that pilgrimage he was a follower of Abu Bakr in his leadership..." to the end of what he said, there is no doubt that the rulings with which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Ali were a specific matter—namely, the recitation of the verses of *Bara’ah* and the other four matters attached to them. However, the point is that the word of revelation—"None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you"—is not restricted in its indication to the discharge of the verses of *Bara’ah*, as previously explained. Thus, one should not mix between what the word indicates and what Ali was commanded with in that specific journey.

As for his saying: "and he was in that pilgrimage a follower...", it is a matter derived from the speech of Abu Hurayrah and its likes, and the discussion regarding it has already passed.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it *hasan*), Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Anas رضي الله عنه, who said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent *Bara’ah* with Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه, then he called him and said: 'It is not fitting for anyone to convey this except a man from my family (أهلي).' So he called Ali and gave it to him."

**I say:** The author of *al-Manar* mentioned in some of his discourse: "The saying of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'or a man from me' in the narration of al-Suddi has been interpreted by other narrations in al-Tabari and elsewhere by his (ص) saying: 'or a man from the people of my house (أهل بيتي).' This explicit text (النص الصريح) invalidates the interpretation of the word 'from me' (مني) as meaning that Ali's soul is like the soul of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and that he is like him and superior to all his companions." [End of quote].

What he pointed to of the narrations is what he narrated previously by saying: "Ahmad recorded with a good chain from Anas that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent *Bara’ah* with Abu Bakr, and when he reached Dhu al-Hulayfah, he said: 'None shall convey it except I or a man from the people of my house,' so he sent it with Ali."

This is exactly—as is not hidden—the previous narration we cited from Anas, though the wording of the two narrations differs due to the hand of transmission by meaning (النقل بالمعنى).

The first point in his discourse is that the wording "or a man from me" did not occur only in a single suspended narration—the narration of al-Suddi which he weakened shortly before. Rather, the basis for that is the word of revelation established by the majority of authentic narrations despite their great number. The other narrations containing the phrase "from the people of my house"—which he considers numerous—are only the narration of Anas, as far as we have found, and in some of its wordings, the phrase "from my family" (من أهلي) occurs in place of "from the people of my house" (من أهل بيتي).

Second: The narration—as has become clear to you—is transmitted by meaning. Despite this, some of the wordings occurring in it are not suitable to interpret what the majority of authentic narrations from the paths of both groups have agreed upon regarding the word of revelation reported in them.

Furthermore, if his saying "from the people of my house" in this narration were suitable to interpret what occurred in other narrations of the wording "a man from you" or "a man from me," then what occurred in the previous narration of Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri—his عليه‌السلام saying: "O Ali, indeed none shall discharge on my behalf except I or you"—would be an interpretation of what is in the narration of Anas: "except a man from the people of my house" or "except a man from my family," and what is in the other narrations: "except a man from you" or "except a man from me."

Thus, these wordings return to being a metonymy (كناية) for the person of Ali عليه‌السلام; rather, the metonymy with its meaning points to him being from the soul of the Prophet عليه‌السلام, from his family, and from the people of his house altogether. This is the very thing he fled from and more.

Third: Deriving that he عليه‌السلام is in the position of his [the Prophet's] own soul عليه‌السلام is not based solely on his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying "a man from me," as he assumed. For the mere saying of a speaker "so-and-so is from me" does not indicate placing him in his position in all affairs of his existence and his being identical to him, but rather indicates a type of connection and following, as in the saying of Abraham عليه‌السلام: **فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي** (So whoever follows me, he is indeed of me) [14:36], unless there is a type of context indicating a rhetorical intent, like His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** (And whoever among you takes them as allies, he is indeed one of them) [5:51].

Rather, that was derived from his saying "a man from me" or "a man from you" with the aid of his saying "None shall discharge on your behalf except you," based on the explanation that preceded. On this basis, if there were a saying: "None shall discharge on my behalf except a man from my family or a man from the people of my house," the very same thing would be derived from it as was derived from his saying "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you" and his saying "None shall discharge on my behalf except I or a man from me." This is in addition to the fact that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله counted him as being from him in his address to Abu Bakr, while he [Abu Bakr] is also from him by following.

Fourth: He ignored in the research the authentic, profuse, or mutawatir narrations indicating that the "People of the House" (أهل البيت) of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله are: Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans, as preceded in the reports of the verse of *Mubahalah* and as most of them will come in the reports of the verse of *Tathir*, if Allah wills.

And there is no man among the people of his house صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله except Ali عليه‌السلام, so the matter results in the wording being a metonymy for Ali عليه‌السلام, returning to the previously mentioned point.

As for the meaning he conjectured—that the intent of "the people of his house" is his general relatives from the Banu Hashim, or the Banu Hashim and his wives—it reduces the wording to an ordinary level without carrying any merit, the meaning being: "None shall discharge the repudiation of the treaty on my behalf except a man from the Banu Hashim." These people often return in the concepts of such wordings to what the linguistic convention gives, without paying attention to what the Sharia has considered. Similar to this has preceded in the meaning of "son" and "daughter," where they assumed that whether the daughter's son is a man's son or not is a matter of linguistic research determining whether "son" applies according to linguistic placement to the daughter's son, for example, or does not apply to him. All of this returns to the mixing between linguistic research and conceptual research, as well as the mixing between social perspectives and heavenly religious perspectives, as previously indicated.

Most amazing of all is his saying: "And this explicit text invalidates the interpretation of the word 'from me'," for his intent, by the indication of the context, is that the phrase "from the people of my house" is an explicit text that the intent of "a man from me" is a man from the Banu Hashim. We do not know what "explicitness" or "clarity" the phrase "People of the House" has regarding the Banu Hashim after the narrations have multiplied that the People of the House of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله are Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans عليه‌السلام; then in his saying: "my People of the House" in the sense of the Banu Hashim, that the intent of the word "from me" is that!

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far and Abu Abd Allah عليه‌السلام: **فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ** (So travel the land for four months). He said: "Twenty days of *Dhu al-Hijjah*, *al-Muharram*, *Safar*, *Rabi‘ al-Awwal*, and ten days of *Rabi‘ al-Akhar*."

**I say:** The narrations from the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام have become profuse that the intent of the four months is this. This was narrated by al-Kulayni, al-Saduq, al-Ayyashi, and al-Qummi in their books. It was also narrated through Sunni paths. There are other narrations from their paths with a different meaning, to the point that it occurred in some of them that Abu Bakr performed the pilgrimage with the people in the ninth year in the month of *Dhu al-Qa‘dah*, which is not supported, and therefore we have overlooked it.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Hakim ibn Jubayr from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَذانٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** (And a proclamation from Allah and His Messenger). He said: "The proclamation (الأذان) is the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام."

**I say:** This meaning was also narrated from Hariz from Abu Abd Allah عليه‌السلام, and from Jabir from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad and Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام. Al-Qummi narrated it from his father from Fadalah from Aban ibn Uthman from Hakim ibn Jubayr from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام, who said: "And in another hadith he said: 'I was the proclamation among the people.'" Al-Saduq also narrated it with his chain from Hakim from him عليه‌السلام. It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Hatim from Hakim ibn Humayd from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام. It is stated in *Tafsir al-Burhan*: "Al-Suddi, Abu Malik, Ibn Abbas, and Zayn al-Abidin said: 'The proclamation is Ali ibn Abi Talib, who discharged it.'"

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Saduq with his chain from al-Fudayl ibn ‘Iyad from Abu Abd Allah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the Great Pilgrimage (الحج الأكبر). He said: 'Do you have something regarding it?' I said: 'Yes, Ibn Abbas used to say: The Great Pilgrimage is the Day of Arafah—meaning that whoever reaches the Day of Arafah until the rising of the sun on the Day of Sacrifice has reached the pilgrimage, and whoever misses that has missed the pilgrimage—so he made the night of Arafah for what is before it and what is after it. The proof for that is that whoever reaches the night of the Sacrifice until the rising of the dawn has reached the pilgrimage, and it suffices him for Arafah.'"

Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'The Great Pilgrimage (الحج الأكبر) is the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر).'" He argued using the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **"Go about freely in the land for four months"** (9:2). These are twenty days of *Dhu al-Hijjah*, *al-Muharram*, *Safar*, *Rabi‘ al-Awwal*, and ten days of *Rabi‘ al-Thani*. Had the Great Pilgrimage been the Day of *‘Arafah*, the period of free movement would have been four months and one day. He also argued using His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **"An announcement from Allah and His Apostle to the people on the day of the Great Pilgrimage"** (9:3), [saying]: "And I was the announcer (الأذان) among the people."

I asked: "What is the meaning of this expression: 'The Great Pilgrimage'?" He replied: "It was only called 'Great' because it was a year in which both Muslims and polytheists performed the pilgrimage, and no polytheist performed the pilgrimage after that year."

In the same source, narrated from him with his chain of transmission from Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Day of the Great Pilgrimage." He replied: "The Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر), and the 'Lesser' [Pilgrimage] is the *‘Umrah*."

**I say:** In this narration, in addition to interpreting the "Day" as the Day of Sacrifice, there is an indication of the reason for naming the pilgrimage "Great." The narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) have reached a consensus—except for rare exceptions—that the intended meaning of the "Day of the Great Pilgrimage" in the verse is the Day of *al-Adha*, the tenth of *Dhu al-Hijjah*, which is the Day of Sacrifice. They narrated this from ‘Ali (peace be upon him).

Al-Kulayni narrated this report in *al-Kafi* from ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him). He also narrated it with his chain from Dharih from him (peace be upon him). Likewise, al-Saduq narrated it with his chain to Dharih from him (peace be upon him), and al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from ‘Abd al-Rahman, Ibn Udhaynah, and al-Fudayl ibn ‘Iyad from him (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abi Awfa from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he said on the Day of *al-Adha*: "This is the Day of the Great Pilgrimage."

In the same source, al-Bukhari extracted as a *mu‘allaq* report, and Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu‘aym in *al-Hilyah* extracted from Ibn ‘Umar: "That the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) stood on the Day of Sacrifice between the *Jamarat* during the pilgrimage he performed and said: 'What day is this?' They said: 'The Day of Sacrifice.' He said: 'This is the Day of the Great Pilgrimage.'"

**I say:** This has been narrated through various chains from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), Ibn ‘Abbas, Mughirah ibn Shu‘bah, Abu Juhayfah, and ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa. It has also been narrated through other various chains from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that it is the Day of *‘Arafah*; similarly, this was narrated from ‘Ali, Ibn ‘Abbas, and Ibn al-Zubayr. It was narrated from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib that it is the day following the Day of Sacrifice. It was also narrated that it refers to all the days of the pilgrimage, and narrated that it was the pilgrimage in the year Abu Bakr led the Hajj. This last view is not incompatible with what was previously mentioned in the hadith from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): that it was called the Great Pilgrimage because both Muslims and polytheists performed the pilgrimage together in that year.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **"Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you find them"** (9:5). He said: "It is from the Day of Sacrifice until ten days have passed of the month of *Rabi‘ al-Thani*."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and pay the zakat"** (9:5). Al-Hakim extracted and authenticated from Mus‘ab ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman from his father (may Allah be pleased with him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) conquered Mecca, then departed for al-Ta’if and besieged them for eight or seven [days]. Then he traveled morning and evening, then halted, then emigrated."

Then he said: "O people, I am your predecessor (فرط), and I enjoin you to treat my progeny (عترتي) well; your meeting place is the Pond (الحوض). By Him in whose hand is my soul, you shall surely maintain the prayer and pay the *zakat*, or I shall surely send against you a man from me—or like my own self—who shall strike the necks of their fighters and take captive their progeny." The people thought he meant Abu Bakr or ‘Umar (may Allah be pleased with them both), but he took the hand of ‘Ali (may Allah be pleased with him) and said: "This one."

**I say:** He (peace be upon him and his progeny) meant by it [the threat of] disbelief (الكفر).

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, in a hadith of Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): **"Yet if they repent"** (9:11) means: "If they believe, then they are your brothers in religion."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"If any of the polytheists seeks asylum with you, grant him asylum"** (9:6) the verse—he said: "He said: 'Recite [the Quran] to him and make it known to him, then do not interfere with him until he returns to his place of safety.'"

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahrashub from the *Tafsir* of al-Qushayri: "A man said to ‘Ali: 'O son of Abu Talib, if one of us wishes to meet the Messenger regarding some matter after the expiration of the four [months], does he have no covenant?' ‘Ali said: 'Yes [he does], for Allah said: **"If any of the polytheists seeks asylum with you, grant him asylum..."**'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"But if they break their pledges after their covenant"** (9:12) the verse: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Hudhayfah (may Allah be pleased with him) that this verse was mentioned in his presence, and he said: "The people of this verse have not been fought yet."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Zayd ibn Wahb regarding His saying: **"then fight the leaders of unfaith"** (9:12). He said: "We were with Hudhayfah (may Allah be pleased with him) and he said: 'There remain only three of the people of this verse, and only four of the hypocrites.' A Bedouin said: 'You are the companions of Muhammad; you tell us things we do not understand. What about these people who break into our houses and steal our precious belongings?' He replied: 'Those are the transgressors (الفساق). Indeed, only four of them remain, one of whom is a very old man; if he were to drink cold water, he would not feel its coldness.'"

In *Qurb al-Isnad* by al-Himyari: ‘Abd al-Hamid and ‘Abd al-Samad ibn Muhammad both narrated to me from Hannan ibn Sadir, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'Some people from Basra came to me and asked me about Talhah and al-Zubayr. I said to them: "They were among the leaders of unfaith (أئمة الكفر). On the day of Basra, when ‘Ali lined up the horses, he said to his companions: 'Do not rush the people until I have offered an excuse between myself and Allah, and between them.'"'

He stood before them and said: 'O people of Basra, do you find any injustice (جور) in my judgment?' They said: 'No.' He said: 'Any unfairness (حيف) in distribution?' They said: 'No.' He said: 'Or a desire for the world that I took for myself and my family to your exclusion, such that you resented me and broke my allegiance?' They said: 'No.' He said: 'Did I establish the legal punishments (الحدود) among you while suspending them for others?' They said: 'No.' He said: 'Then why is my allegiance broken while the allegiance of others is not? I have examined this matter from every angle and found nothing but disbelief (الكفر) or the sword.'

Then he turned to his companions and said: 'Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says in His Book: **"But if they break their pledges after their covenant and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed, there is no [validity to] their pledges—so that they may desist"** (9:12).' Then the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'By Him who split the grain and created the soul, and chose Muhammad for prophethood, they are indeed the people of this verse, and they have not been fought since it was revealed.'"

**I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from Hannan ibn Sadir from him (peace be upon him).

In the *Amali* of al-Mufid, with his chain from Abu ‘Uthman, the muezzin of Banu Qusay, who said: "I heard ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) when Talhah and al-Zubayr came out to fight him, saying: 'Allah has excused me regarding Talhah and al-Zubayr; they pledged allegiance to me willingly, without compulsion, then they broke my allegiance without any cause I provided.' Then he recited this verse: **"But if they break their pledges after their covenant and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed, there is no [validity to] their pledges—so that they may desist"** (9:12)." **I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Abu ‘Uthman the muezzin, Abu al-Tufayl, and al-Hasan al-Basri similarly. Al-Shaykh narrated it in his *Amali* from Abu ‘Uthman the muezzin. In his hadith, Bukayr said: "I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) about it, and he said: 'The old man spoke the truth; thus said ‘Ali, and thus it was.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Ishaq and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* extracted from Marwan ibn al-Hakam and al-Miswar ibn Makhramah, who said: "It was part of the peace treaty of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) on the day of al-Hudaybiyyah between him and the Quraysh that whoever wished to enter into the contract (عقد) and covenant (عهد) of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) could do so, and whoever wished to enter into the covenant and contract of the Quraysh could do so. The Khuza‘ah stepped forward and said: 'We enter into the covenant and contract of Muhammad.' The Banu Bakr stepped forward and said: 'We enter into the contract and covenant of the Quraysh.' They remained in that truce for about seventeen or eighteen months.

Then the Banu Bakr, who had entered into the contract and covenant of the Quraysh, attacked the Khuza‘ah, who had entered into the contract and covenant of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), by night at a watering place of theirs called al-Watir, near Mecca. The Quraysh said: 'Muhammad does not know about us, and this is night, and no one sees us.' So they aided them with horses and weapons, and fought alongside them out of malice toward the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny).

‘Amr ibn Salim rode, when the matter of Khuza‘ah and Banu Bakr occurred at al-Watir, until he reached the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) in Medina with verses he recited to him:

O Lord, I am adjuring Muhammad
By the ancient alliance of our father and his.
You were children and we were parents,
Then we submitted and did not withdraw our hand.
So grant victory, may Allah guide you, a prepared victory,
And call the servants of Allah, they will come as reinforcements.
Among them is the Messenger of Allah, ready for action,
If he is treated with injustice, his face darkens with anger.
In an army like the sea, flowing with foam.
Indeed, the Quraysh have broken their promise to you,
And violated your confirmed treaty.
They set an ambush for me at Kada’,
And claimed that I would not call upon anyone,
While they are more humiliated and fewer in number.
They attacked us at al-Watir while we were sleeping,
And killed us while bowing and prostrating.

The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: 'You have been granted victory, O ‘Amr ibn Salim.' He had not left before a cloud passed in the sky, and the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: 'Indeed, this cloud bears witness to the victory of the Banu Ka‘b.' The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) ordered the people to Jihad and kept his destination secret, asking Allah to blind the Quraysh to news of him so that he might surprise them in their lands."

**I say:** This narration was cited in *al-Durr al-Manthur* after what was narrated through chains from Mujahid and ‘Ikrimah: that the story of the Quraysh breaking the Hudaybiyyah covenant and their aiding Banu Bakr against Khuza‘ah—the allies of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny)—was the reason for the revelation of His saying, the Exalted: **"Will you not fight a people..."** (9:13) until His saying: **"and soothe the breasts of a faithful people"** (9:14), who were the Khuza‘ah.

If the matter were as they mentioned, the verse: **"Will you not fight a people who broke their pledges..."** (9:13) until the end of three or even four verses—as the context suggests—would have been revealed before the conquest of Mecca, and thus necessarily before the verses of *Bara’ah*.

However, the story narrated by Ibn Ishaq and al-Bayhaqi, despite its consideration due to al-Miswar ibn Makhramah, does not explicitly state the revelation of the verses regarding that event. As for what Mujahid and ‘Ikrimah narrated, it cannot be relied upon due to its being *waqf* (stopped) and *inqita‘* (disconnected). The context of the verses does not preclude their revelation along with what preceded them and their connection to it, as is not hidden.

What is mentioned therein of His saying: **"broke their pledges and they conspired to expel the Apostle, and it was they who began [hostilities] with you the first time"** (9:13), although it points to the specific characteristics of the Quraysh, it is possible that the verse refers to the allies and neighbors of the Quraysh who had not yet believed after the conquest of Mecca. Due to their unity and connection with the Quraysh, they were described with what the Quraysh were originally described with.

Know that there are scattered narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that apply these verses to the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), which is a matter of *al-jary* (the application of verses to subsequent instances).

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of Covenant, its Categories, and its Rulings)

We previously presented at the beginning of the sixth volume of this book a discourse on the meaning of contract (العقد) and covenant (العهد). We now resume the explanation here regarding the meaning of what has preceded and the categories and rulings that follow it, in a different presentation across several sections:

1. It has become clear to you from the preceding discussions in this book that man, in the course of his life, constantly conceptualizes his actions and the material things related to them as if they were cosmic realities (أمور كونية), representing them as such and applying to them the rulings and effects of cosmic realities—the general laws governing the universe—according to what suits his vital purposes. For instance, he takes separate sounds like *Zay*, *Ya*, and *Dal*, composes them in a specific form, and creates the word "Zayd." He then assumes this is the external human being, Zayd, and names him by it. Whenever he wishes to bring Zayd to the mind of his listener, he presents the word "Zayd," which then represents the very essence of Zayd to the listener, thereby achieving his purpose.

If he wishes to manage an affair that can only be accomplished by a coordinated group of people, he selects a group and assumes them to be a single entity, like a single human being. He designates one of them for the rest as the head (الرأس) is designated for the human body, calling him a leader (رئيسا). He designates each of the others as a limb (عضوا) of the multi-limbed body. Then, he applies to the "head" the rulings of an external head, and to the "limb" the effects of an external limb, and so on.

To this end, all social thoughts of man—whether directly or through one or more intermediaries of concepts and assertions—return when analyzed correctly, just as his individual views regarding his actions and deeds return to it.

Man is intensely concerned with concluding contracts (عقد العقود) and representing covenants (تمثيل العهود) and what relates to them of oaths (الحلف), swearing (اليمين), allegiance (البيعة), and the like. The primary factor in this is that man has no concern other than the preservation of his life, attaining its advantages, and enjoying the happiness that follows if life proceeds according to its true course.

Whatever objective he finds and gains power over, he begins to enjoy it in a manner suitable to it, such as eating, drinking, and other things, using the tools of enjoyment with which he is equipped. He repels everything that prevents him from enjoyment if an obstacle arises, seeing that he only succeeded in this in light of the power (السلطة) granted to him.

Man has been granted the commodity of thought (الفكر), by which he manages his life and rectifies his livelihood, working for his today and preparing for his tomorrow. His actions—which are his manipulations of matter or related to it—while all dependent on the extension of his power over the act and his encompassment of everything related to his work, differ in that some are completed by power restricted to the act for the duration of its time, such as one who finds food while hungry and consumes it. This does not depend on a power broader than the time of the act, nor on preparation or a preamble.

Others—which constitute the majority of social human actions—depend on a broad power that extends over the act at its time and over a time before it only, or a time before and after it, due to the need for preambles he prepares and prior management he provides for its existence. Not every act man performs is by chance; rather, most vital matters are such that man prepares for them before their time.

Part of this preparation is to prepare for gathering its causes and organizing the means by which he reaches it, and to prepare for removing its obstacles that would interfere with its existence and occurrence. Thus, man does not succeed in an act nor prosper in his endeavor unless he is secure from losing the causes or facing obstacles and interferences.

Awareness of this reality is what prompted man to seek security from his rivals in life: that they assist him in matters requiring a partner-assistant, or that they do not hinder him from action in matters depending on the removal and cessation of obstacles.

Thus, when a man wishes to make a garment to wear from a simple material like cotton or wool, the matter depends on many actions performed by the spinner, the weaver, the tailor, and those who make the tools for spinning, weaving, and tailoring. His desire to obtain the garment will not be fulfilled, nor will his endeavor succeed, unless he is secure from these rivals: that they work according to what he desires and do not leave him alone, causing his endeavor to fail and his work to be lost.

Similarly, a person residing in a land or living in a house cannot complete his residence except with security from people's hindrance and their interference with his residence and his disposal of it in a way that makes it suitable for that purpose.

This is what guided man to the consideration of the contract (العقد) and the firming of the covenant (العهد). He takes the action he desires and links it to the action of another that assists him, "tying" them together—representing by this the tying of ropes (عقد الحبال) which results in the connection of parts and their not failing one another. Similar to this is the covenant (العهد) that another pledges to him: to assist him in what he desires or not to hinder him in it.

To this return all general contracts, such as the marriage contract (عقد النكاح), the contract of buying and selling (عقد البيع والشري), and the lease contract (عقد الإجارة). The term "covenant" (العهد) in its general sense applies to them, which is for a person to give another a word or a writing that he will assist him in such-and-half or not prevent him from such-and-such for a fixed term or indefinitely.

The discussion here is regarding the covenant (العهد) that is not specified by a particular name like the contract of sale, marriage, and other transactional contracts; these are outside our purpose and have specific rulings, effects, and properties in human societies. Rather, the discussion is on the covenant in the sense of what a person pledges to another of assistance or non-hindrance in various social objectives, and the effects he establishes for that. For example, one who covenants with another to give him a certain amount of wealth every year to assist him in his needs, and takes from him a certain amount of wealth or benefit, or covenants not to interfere with his work or not to hinder his path until a certain time or indefinitely. This is a type of firming and strengthening that is not broken except by the breach of one of the parties or both.

Sometimes the firming of the covenant is increased by an oath (الحلف), which is for the covenanter to restrict the covenant he gives and link it to a matter of great significance that he sanctifies and respects. It is as if he makes its sanctity and honor a pledge (رهنا) for his covenant, representing that if he breaks it, he has forfeited its sanctity. The covenanter says: "By Allah, I shall not betray you," "By my life, I shall surely assist you," and "I swear I shall surely help you." He represents by this that if he breaks his promise and violates his covenant, he has nullified the sanctity of his Lord, or the sanctity of his life, or the sanctity of his oath, and thus has no manliness (مروة).

Sometimes the covenant and pact are firmed by allegiance (البيعة) and the striking of a deal (الصفقة), where the covenanter places his hand in the hand of the one he covenants with, representing that he has given him his hand—by which he performs his actions—so he will not do what his partner dislikes, because his hand is in the grasp of the other's hand.

2. Covenants and pacts (العهود والمواثيق) affect the life of society just as they affect the life of the individual who is a member of society. Society is nothing but the collection of human individuals; its life is the sum of the lives of its parts, and its vital actions are the sum of the actions of its parts. It possesses good and evil, benefit and harm, health and sickness, growth and maturity, rectitude and deviation, happiness and misery, and persistence and cessation, all of which are the sum of what its parts possess.

Thus, society is a "large human" (إنسان كبير) possessing the same life objectives as the "small human." The relationship of one society to another is close to the relationship of one individual to another. It requires, in pursuing its objectives and performing its actions, security and peace similar to what the individual requires. In fact, the need in society is more intense and powerful because the action becomes great by the greatness of its doer and the greatness of its purpose. Society is in need of security and peace from its own parts so that it does not dissolve and scatter, and security and peace from its rivals among other societies.

Upon this has proceeded the custom of human societies according to the history of past nations and peoples in our hands, and what we hear or witness of present nations. A society among human societies has never ceased, nor will it cease, to be in constant need of covenanting with others in some of its political, economic, cultural, or other life affairs. The atmosphere does not clear for embarking on any life objective or progressing in any of its goals except by seeking support from allies and security from the opposition of obstacles.

3. Islam, since it addresses the affair of society as it does the individual, and is concerned with rectifying the general life of people as it is concerned with rectifying the private life of the individual, has legislated the universals of what pertains to social life affairs, such as Jihad, defense, fighting the people of rebellion and breach, peace, truce, covenants, pacts, and so on.

The covenant (العهد) we are discussing has been given full consideration and firmed with a strengthening such that its breach by its people is considered among the greatest sins, unless the other covenanter breaks it, in which case he is met with the like. For Allah, the Glorified, commanded the fulfillment of covenants and contracts and severely censured the breaking of covenants and pacts in very many verses. The Exalted said: **"O you who have faith! Keep your agreements"** (5:1). And He said: **"But those who break the covenant of Allah after its confirmation... for such shall be the curse, and for them shall be the ills of the [ultimate] abode"** (13:25). And He said: **"And fulfill the covenant; indeed, all covenants are accountable"** (17:34), among others.

He did not permit the breaking of covenants and pacts except where the right of justice permits it, which is when the opposing covenanter breaks it through rebellion and arrogance, or when his fulfillment cannot be trusted because he has fallen below the level of credibility. This is something against which no objector can object nor any censurer blame. The Exalted said: **"And if you fear treachery from a people, break off [the covenant] with them in like manner. Indeed, Allah does not like the treacherous"** (8:58). Thus, He permitted the breaking of the covenant upon the fear of treachery, but He did not approve of the breach without informing them of it and assassinating them while they are unaware; rather, He said: **"break off with them in like manner,"** making it obligatory to inform them of the reciprocal breach to avoid the vice of treachery.

He said: **"[This is] a declaration of immunity from Allah and His Apostle to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty. Go about freely in the land for four months"** (9:1-2). He did not approve of the immunity without granting them a respite of four months so they might have leisure to reflect on their affair and deliberate on their situation, seeing their own view with freedom of thought. If they wished, they could believe and be saved; if they did not wish, they would be killed and perish. The good effect of this delay was that they believed and did not perish.

The Glorified completed this benefit in the best manner by His saying after the announcement of immunity: **"If any of the polytheists seeks asylum with you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah, then convey him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know"** (9:6).

He said, exempting those among the polytheists who fulfilled their covenant: **"How can the polytheists have a treaty with Allah and His Apostle, except those with whom you made a treaty at the Holy Mosque? So long as they are true to you, be true to them. Indeed, Allah loves the God-conscious. How? [For] if they get the better of you, they will observe toward you neither kinship nor treaty. They please you with their mouths while their hearts reject [you], and most of them are transgressors"** (9:7-8). He justified the steadfastness toward those who remained steadfast by stating it is part of godwariness (التقوى)—that godwariness to which there is no call in religion except toward it—and that Allah loves the God-conscious. This is a living justification until the Day of Resurrection.

The Exalted said: **"So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you"** (2:194). And He said: **"And do not let ill will toward a people because they barred you from the Holy Mosque incite you to transgress. Help one another in piety and godwariness, and do not help one another in sin and aggression"** (5:2).

As for the initial breach without a breach from the covenanted enemy, there is no permission for it in this upright religion at all. His saying, the Exalted, has preceded: **"So long as they are true to you, be true to them,"** and He said: **"and do not transgress. Indeed, Allah does not like the transgressors"** (2:190).

Upon this proceeded the practice of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) during his life. He covenanted with Banu Qaynuqa‘, Banu Qurayzah, and other Jews, and did not break it until after they broke it. He covenanted with the Quraysh at al-Hudaybiyyah and did not break it until they broke it by aiding Banu Bakr against Khuza‘ah, while Khuza‘ah were in the covenant of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) and Banu Bakr were in the covenant of the Quraysh.

As for a breach without a breach [from the other side], there is no permit for it in Islam, even if fulfillment causes the Muslims to lose some of their benefits or brings some harm upon them, while they have the power to preserve their benefits through might and strength, or could excuse themselves with some apparent argument that might present a justification and avert blame. For the pivot of the matter is upon the Truth (الحق), and the Truth does not result in evil or harm except for those who deviate from it and seek refuge in something else.

4. Human societies—especially the advanced and civilized ones other than the religious society—have no goal for their gathering nor any purpose for their current customs except to enjoy the advantages of material life as much as they are able. Thus, they have no motive to preserve anything beyond the practical laws in their hands that organize their various vital objectives.

It is necessary that in a context of this nature, spiritual values (المعنويات) have no value except to the extent that they agree with material vital objectives. Spiritual virtues and vices—such as truthfulness, chivalry, manliness, spreading mercy, compassion, kindness, and the like—have no consideration except to the extent that they yield benefits for society and do not cause harm if they were not considered. As for what contradicts the interests of the people, there is no motive to act upon them; rather, the motive is for their opposite.

Therefore, you see official conferences and the guardians of affairs in societies seeing no duty for themselves except the preservation of the vital interests of society. The covenants and pacts concluded therein are only concluded according to the interest of the time, and are weighed by the scale of the strength and preparation of the covenanting state, and the strength and preparation of the opposing covenanter in himself and what is added to him of other joined requirements that assist him.

As long as the balance is in a state of equilibrium, the covenant remains as it is. If the scale tips in favor of the covenanting state over its opponent, it nullifies the consideration of the covenant with manufactured excuses and fabricated accusations to seek a way to break it. The presentation of excuses is only intended to preserve the appearance of international laws, the breach of which and deviation from which would result in nothing but a threat to the life of society or some of its vital interests. Were it not for that, there would be nothing to prevent the breach, even without an excuse, if the vital interests of the powerful society required it.

As for lying, treachery, or transgression against what others take as benefits for themselves, it is not what prevents a society among societies from acquiring what it sees as beneficial for its affair. For ethics and spiritual values have no ontological independence (أصالة) for them; they are only considered according to what the goal of society and its vital purpose—which is the enjoyment of life—estimates.

If you trace the general events between societies, past and present—especially the current global events in this recent era—you will find a great deal of firmed covenants and their breaches according to what we have described.

As for Islam, it does not consider the material life of man to be his true life, nor the enjoyment of its advantages to be his real happiness. Rather, it sees his true life as the life that combines the material and the spiritual, and his real happiness that must be secured as that which makes him happy in his world and his hereafter.

This necessitates that the laws of life be built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and creation, rather than what man considers suitable for his own state. It establishes its true call upon following the Truth and being guided by it, rather than following caprice (الهوى) and emulating what the majority incline toward with their emotions and inner feelings. The Exalted said: **"So set your heart on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion"** (30:30). And He said: **"It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the religion of truth (دين الحق) that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse"** (9:33). And He said: **"Rather, We have brought them the truth"** (23:90). And He said: **"Had the truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen into ruin, and whoever is in them"** (23:71).

Among the requirements of this is that the right of belief, the virtue of character, and the righteousness of action must all be observed. Matter is not independent of meaning, nor is meaning independent of matter. It is obligatory to observe the side of human virtues whether they benefit or harm, and to avoid vices whether they benefit or harm, because that is part of following the Truth. Far be it from the Truth to cause harm except to those who deviate from its scale and overstep what the Truth has outlined for them.

From here, we see that Allah, the Glorified, breaks the covenant of the polytheists because of their breach of His covenant, yet He employs mercy by granting them a respite of four months. He commands steadfastness toward those among the polytheists who remained steadfast in their covenant, even though events had humiliated them then and they had weakened without the power of Islam. Likewise, He commands His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), if he fears treachery from a people, to break their covenant, but He commands him to inform them of that and justifies it by stating that He does not love treachery.

## (A Discourse on the Attribution of Actions to Causes in a Longitudinal Manner)

It has been mentioned in various places in this book that what rational research yields is that events, just as they have an attribution to their proximate causes connected to them, also have an attribution to their ultimate causes which are the causes of these causes. Thus, events are acts that, while being among the acts of their proximate causes directly performing the work, the act—such as motion, for example—depends on its moving agent and depends on the mover of its mover in the same way it depends on its mover. This is similar to one gear moving another which moves a third; it is not a motion by accident (بالعرض).

Thus, the act has an attribution to its agent, and it has an attribution to the agent of the agent by the very same attribution it has to its agent, not by another separate attribution independent in itself. However, when it is attributed to the agent of the agent, the proximate agent becomes like an instrument (الآلة) in relation to the agent of the agent—that is, a mere intermediary with no independence in the work, in the sense that it is not independent in its effect from the agent of the agent. For the assumption of the latter's absence is synonymous with the non-existence of the agent and the non-existence of its effect.

It is not a condition of the intermediary that it be devoid of consciousness of its act or devoid of choice. For the consciousness by which the conscious agent affects its act was not created by the agent for itself; rather, it was created in it by its agent who created the agent and its consciousness. Likewise, choice (الاختيار) was not created by the choosing agent for itself; rather, it was created by the agent who created the choosing agent. Just as the act in non-conscious and non-voluntary cases depends on its agent and depends by this very dependence on the agent of the agent, so too the conscious act and the voluntary act depend on their agent and depend by this very dependence on the agent of the agent who created for the agent consciousness and choice.

Thus, the agent of the conscious or choosing agent intended for the conscious or choosing agent to perform such-and-such an act through its consciousness or to perform such-and-half a voluntary act by its choice. The act was intended through the path of choice because the act was intended, and the choice through which its agent appeared was not neglected. Understand this, so that a foot does not slip after being firm.

Upon this reality, people proceed according to their instinctive understanding, attributing the act to the remote cause just as they attribute it to the proximate cause directly performing it, insofar as it is an effect emanating from it. It is said: "So-and-so built a house" and "dug a well," while it was the builder and the digger who directly performed it. It is said: "The prince lashed so-and-so," "killed so-and-so," "captured so-and-so," and "fought such-and-such a people," while it was his executioner who directly performed the lashing, his swordsman the killing, his guards the capturing, and his soldiers the fighting. It is said: "So-and-so burned the garment of so-and-so," while it was the fire that burned it; and "So-and-so healed such-and-such a patient," while it was the medicine he handed him and ordered him to drink and use that healed him.

In all of this, the command of the commander or the seeking of the seeker is considered an effect from him upon the proximate agent, and then the act attributed to the proximate agent is attributed to the remote agent. The basis of the attribution is nothing but a literal attribution (نسبة حقيقة) without any metaphor (مجاز) whatsoever.

Whoever among the scholars of literature and others said that all of this is a metaphor in the word (مجاز في الكلمة) because of the validity of negating the act from the remote agent—for the owner of the building did not place one brick upon another, as that is the affair of the builder who directly performed the work!—only intended the act with the specificity of its issuance from the direct agent. It is accepted that direct performance (المباشرة) is the affair of the proximate agent, and we have no dispute in that. The discussion is rather regarding what is conceptualized for it of existence that depends on a creative agent (فاعل موجد). This meaning, just as it subsists in the direct agent, also subsists by this very subsistence in the agent of the agent.

The consideration of this point is what necessitated for them to distinguish between actions and attribute some to both the proximate and remote agents together, while attributing others only to the proximate agent directly performing the work. Those actions which, by their concept, reveal the specificities of direct performance and connection to the work—such as eating in the sense of taking and swallowing, drinking in the sense of sipping and gulping, and sitting in the sense of taking a seat, and the like—are not attributed except to the direct agent. If a master orders his servant to eat such-and-such food, drink such-and-such a drink, and sit on such-and-such a chair, it is said: "The servant ate, drank, and sat," and it is not said: "His master ate it, drank it, and sat on it." Rather, it is said: "He disposed of such-and-such" if he used such-and-such or spent such-and-such, and so on, for what we have mentioned.

As for actions in which the specificities of direct performance and the material movements that subsist in the direct agent of the movement are not considered—such as killing, capturing, giving life, causing death, giving, kindness, honoring, and their likes—they are attributed to the proximate and remote agents equally. Indeed, their attribution to the remote agent might be stronger than to the proximate agent if the remote agent is more powerful in existence and more intense in power and encompassment.

This is what rational research yields and what man proceeds upon by his instinctive understanding. The Noble Quran confirms this in the clearest confirmation, such as His saying, the Exalted, in the previous verses: **"Fight them: Allah will punish them by your hands and He will disgrace them, and He will help you against them, and soothe the breasts of a faithful people, and remove the rage of their hearts"** (9:14-15).
