Here, He attributed the punishment—which the hands of the believers directly perform—to Himself, by making their hands like an instrument.

Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **"while Allah has created you and whatever you make"** (37:96). For what is intended by "whatever you make" is either the idols they used to make from stone, wood, or metal—so the creation is intended for the matter along with the work of man upon it, in which there is an attribution of creation to the actions as it is attributed to their agents—or if it refers to the actions themselves, the matter is even clearer.

Close to this is His saying, the Exalted: **"and He has made for you such of the ships and the cattle as you ride"** (43:12). Here, there is an attribution of creation to the ships, and ships are among the works of man.

This is regarding what creation is attributed to of actions issuing from consciousness and will. As for actions that do not depend in their issuance on consciousness and will, such as natural actions, their attribution to Allah, the Glorified, has appeared in very many verses that do not need enumeration, such as giving life to the earth, growing plants, bringing out grain, raining from the sky, flowing rivers, and the sailing of ships that run in the sea by His command, among others.

There is no contradiction in all these instances between the attribution of the matter to Him, the Exalted, and its attribution to others among the natural causes and others. For the attribution is not horizontal (عرضية) such that one of the two attributions crowds out the other; rather, it is longitudinal (طولية), in which there is no problem in its attachment to more than one side.

In the course of our previous discussions, we have already refuted the confusion of the materialists regarding the attribution of general events—such as floods, earthquakes, drought, epidemics, and plague—to Allah, the Glorified, despite the discovery of their natural causes today. They confused horizontal and longitudinal causes and reasons, assuming that their attribution to their natural causes nullifies what the Noble Book has established and what theists have submitted to: their attribution to the Causer of Causes (مسبب الأسباب) to Whom all matters return.

The Ash‘arites and Mu‘tazilites have a strange discussion regarding the previous verse: **"Fight them: Allah will punish them by your hands"** and similar verses, which al-Razi cited in his *Tafsir*; we present it in summary:

He said: The Ash‘arites argued from His saying, the Exalted: **"Fight them: Allah will punish them by your hands"** the verse—that the actions of servants are created by Allah, and that people are compelled (مجبرون) in their actions, not choosing. For Allah, the Glorified, informs therein that He is the one who punishes the polytheists by the killing of some and the wounding of others by the hands of the believers. This indicates that the hands of the believers are like their swords and spears—mere instruments with no effect at all; rather, the act belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and the "acquisition" (الكسب) which is considered the basis for religious obligation is a name without a named reality.

This verse is stronger in its indication of the objective than the indication of His saying, the Exalted: **"And you did not throw when you threw, rather it was Allah who threw"** (8:17). For in that verse, there is an affirmation of the throwing for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny)—even if it was simultaneously negated from him—and an affirmation of its attribution to Allah, the Glorified. But the verse—meaning His saying: **"Fight them: Allah will punish them by your hands"**—is an affirmation of the punishment for Allah, the Glorified, and a making of the hands of the believers which belong to them into instruments in the act, having no effect in it whatsoever.

Al-Jubba’i, from the Mu‘tazilites, replied: If it were permissible to say that Allah punishes the disbelievers by the hands of the believers according to the literal meaning claimed for it, it would be permissible to say that He punishes the believers by the hands of the disbelievers, and that He, the Exalted, belies His prophets by their tongues, and curses the believers and reviles them by their mouths, because He, the Exalted, is the creator of all that. Since that is not permissible, we know that He, the Exalted, did not create the actions of the servants; rather, their actions are the creation of themselves.

By this, it is known that the attribution of punishment in the verse to Him, the Exalted, is a type of figurative extension (توسع), because it only occurred by His command and His grace, just as He, the Exalted, attributes all acts of obedience and good deeds to Himself because of their occurrence by His command and His success (توفيق).

Al-Razi replied to him: Our companions commit to all that al-Jubba’i and his companions necessitated of the necessity of attributing foul deeds to Him, the Exalted. They believe in it inwardly (لبا), even if they do not utter it with the tongue out of etiquette (أدبا) with Allah, the Glorified. [End of summary].

The discussions we have presented in this book around these meanings are sufficient to clarify the truth and illuminate it in this position, and to uncover what both groups fell into.

As for what the Ash‘arites mentioned and committed to, what led them into that was their view of negating the link of causality (العلية والمعلولية) between things and restricting it to between Him, the Exalted, and His creation generally. Thus, there is no cause in existence—neither independently nor through mediation—other than Him, the Exalted. As for the link of causality between things themselves, it is causality in name only, not in reality. It is only the Divine habit (العادة الإلهية) that proceeded by creating what we call "effects" following what we call "causes." Thus, between them and Him, the Exalted, is a real causality, while between themselves, it returns to constant or majority coincidence (الاتفاق).

The consequence of this is the nullification of causality and reason from its root. By its nullification, what they established of the restriction of causality to Him, the Exalted, for everything is nullified. For if it were permissible for the attribution of everything to everything to be a single attribution without difference in affecting and being affected, there would remain nothing for man by which to be alerted to the original meaning of causality. Thus, he would have no way to prove His causality, the Exalted, for everything.

Furthermore, man expects events from other events, is certain of results from their premises, and builds his life upon teaching and education, and upon presenting causes in hope of their effects, whether he acknowledges the Creator or not. None of this is completed for him except through an instinctive submission to the principle of causality. If the human primordial nature permitted the nullification of that and the occurrence of events by mere coincidence, the system of his life would be disrupted by the nullification of his intellectual and practical endeavor, and the path to proving a cause above the nature of events would be blocked.

Moreover, the Noble Book proceeds in its explanations upon the confirmation of the principle of causality. It attributes every good deed to Him, the Exalted, and negates the attribution of evil deeds and sins to Him. It names Him by every most beautiful name and describes Him with every beautiful description, and negates from Him all jest, futility, vanity, play, and randomness. None of this is completed except upon the principle of causality, and what has preceded in the previous discussions clarifies all of that.

A group of materialists—especially the proponents of dialectical materialism—have gone toward the very same thing the Ash‘arites went toward regarding the affirmation of determinism (الجبر) and the negation of choice (الاختيار) from human actions. The only difference between the views of the two groups is that the Ash‘arites built that upon the restricted causality of the Necessary Being, the Exalted, and concluded from that the nullification of voluntary causality and its negation from man. The materialists built it upon the fact that human actions are effects of the sum of events surrounding the act, which are the cause of its occurrence. There is no meaning to causality except by necessity (الإيجاب); thus, man is necessitated in his act, compelled to it.

They missed the fact that what the effect is attributed to by necessity is only the complete cause (العلة التامة), which is the sum of events preceding the effect such that its existence does not depend on anything beyond them. By the existence of all of them, nothing remains for it but to exist. As for some parts of the complete cause, the attribution of the effect to them is by possibility (الإمكان), not by necessity, because its existence depends on other things beyond them. Thus, by the existence of the assumed part, all that its existence depends on is not realized such that its existence becomes necessary.

Human actions depend for their existence on the human being and his will, and on other innumerable matters of material and temporal and spatial conditions. If they are attributed to all of them, the resulting attribution is one of necessity and certainty. But if they are attributed to the human being alone or to the willing human being, they have been attributed to a part of the complete cause, and the attribution returns to possibility rather than necessity. Thus, human voluntary actions are "choosing" (اختيارية)—meaning that he can act and not act. If he acts, it is by his wish and will; and if he does not act, he did not choose it nor intend it, but rather chose and intended something else. However, they do not occur in the external world except as necessary, because they are then attributed to all parts of their causes.

These people confused in their speech between the two attributions. They placed the necessary attribution (النسبة الوجوبية) which the act has to the sum of the parts of its complete cause in the place of the possible attribution (النسبة الإمكانية) which the act has to some parts of its complete cause—which is what is called in the human being "choice" (الاختيار) by way of a certain consideration.

As for the argument raised by the Mu‘tazilites—that if it were permissible for the Exalted to be the [actual] Agent of the act performed by the believers, namely the infliction of punishment, while they hold nothing but the status of pure instruments (الآلية المحضة) without any causal efficacy (تأثير), then it would also be permissible to attribute the disbelievers’ torture of the believers, their denial of the Prophets, and their cursing of the believers to Him as well; and since this is certainly false, the acts of the servants must be created by them, with no hand for Allah the Exalted in them—

The response is that while the logical entailment (الملازمة) is correct, the falsity of the consequent does not necessitate that the acts are created by the servants such that they have no relation to Allah, the Glorified, at all. This is because it is possible for them to be attributed to Him, the Exalted, by the very same means through which they are attributed to the servants. For they are the agents of these acts, and He is the Agent of the agents (فاعل الفاعلين). Thus, the act is attributed to them by virtue of its issuance from the immediate agent (الفاعل المباشر), and it is attributed to Him by virtue of its issuance from the Agent who is the agent of that [immediate] agent. In reality, the two attributions are a single relation that differs only in terms of proximity and distance, and the absence or presence of a medium (الواسطة). This does not necessitate the meeting of two independent agents (فاعلين مستقلين) upon a single act, because they are in a vertical (طوليين) rather than a horizontal (عرضيين) relationship.

**If you say:** The problem of attributing good and evil deeds, as well as faith (الإيمان) and faithlessness (الكفر), to Him, the Exalted, remains in place.

**I say:** Not at all. Rather, what is attributed to Him is the origin of their existence (أصل وجودها). As for the formal title of the act (عنوان الفعل) which points to the manner in which motion or rest subsists in a moving subject—such as marriage (النكاح), adultery (الزنا), or eating what is prohibited or permissible—this is only attributed to the human being because he is the material subject (الموضوع المادي) who moves with these motions. As for the One who brings into existence this moving being—whose motion is among its effects—and is not Himself moving by that motion, but rather brings it into existence (إيجادا) once its conditions and causes are complete, He is not characterized by these types of motions such that He would be characterized by the act of marriage, adultery, or any act subsisting in the human being.

Yes, there are general titles (عناوين عامة) that do not entail the meaning of motion or matter; there is no barrier to attributing these to the human being and to Him, the Glorified, provided it does not lead to an impossibility. Examples include guidance (الهداية) and misguidance (الإضلال)—provided the latter is not an initial misguidance—as well as punishing (التعذيب) and testing (الابتلاء). Thus, the believer’s killing of a disbeliever is a divine punishment (تعذيب إلهي) for the disbeliever, and the disbeliever’s killing of a believer is a fair trial (بلاء حسن) for the believer through which he merits a fair reward from Allah, and so on.

Furthermore, the position adopted by the Mu‘tazilites leads them into the same predicament as the Ash‘arites, which is the closing of the path to proving the existence of the Creator (إثبات الصانع). For if it were permissible for a contingent event (حادث) in the world to be brought into existence by its cause and then be completely severed from what lies beyond that cause—such that the latter has no influence (تأثير) upon it—then it would be permissible for every assumed event to be attributed to its immediate cause without being linked to anything else beyond it. It would then be possible for the agent to perish while its effect remains. Consequently, it would be possible for every assumed effect (معلول) to be attributed to an agent that is not the Necessary Being (واجب الوجود). It would also be possible for every assumed world to be attributed to a preceding world that was its agent and had perished before it, as is witnessed in the events of this world where some give rise to others or are generated from one another. This would not necessitate the impossibility of an infinite regress (التسلسل), because a chain (سلسلة) possessing actual parts does not exist at any single time except in the mind.

There are many other flaws in their discourse which are explained in their relevant place. We have previously mentioned in the discussion regarding the attribution of creation (الخلق) to Him, the Exalted, in the seventh volume of this book, that which is beneficial in this regard.

How can it be permissible for a monotheistic Muslim to affirm another creator alongside Allah, the Glorified, in the true sense of creation (الخلق) and bringing into existence (الإيجاد), when Allah, the Glorified, has said: **ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "That is Allah, your Lord, the Creator of all things, there is no god except Him" (40:62), and He has repeated this throughout His speech? There is no alternative but to attribute the acts of the human being to him without severing their link to Him, the Exalted; rather, the attribution must be affirmed based on the evidence of the verses of predestination (القدر) and the dictate of reason that the act of an agent has an attribution to the Agent of that agent, in a manner befitting His Presence.

The truth, then, is that human acts have an attribution to their immediate agents, and an attribution to Him, the Exalted, in a manner befitting the Presence of His Sanctity. The Exalted said: **كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً** "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (17:20).
